coredata.dc:title: The effect of N and S ratios in N, S co-doped carbon dot inhibitor on metal protection in 1 M HCl solution
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers.
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Jiaxin
Cheng, Xiong
Chen, Xinhua
Zhong, C.F.
Xie, Haolin
Ye, Y.W.
Zhao, H.C.
Li, Yong
Chen, H.
coredata.dc:description:
Background The paper article wants to develop a new type of corrosion inhibitor to replace the traditional corrosion inhibitor and reduce environmental pollution.
Methods A kind of high-performance N, S co-doped carbon dot (N, S-CDs) inhibitor was synthesized by ammonium citrate and methionine with different ratios (100:0, 95:5, 90:10, 85:15). A series of detection equipment were selected to evaluate the structure and corrosion inhibition behavior of as-obtained inhibitors. The result showed that the as-obtained inhibitors could effectively enhance the impedance of steel in acid environment. With the increase of addition amount, the enhancement degree was increased. In the meantime, the icorr of steel was decreased 1∼2 orders of magnitude in acid solution after adding 200 mg/L inhibitor. Among them, the corrosion inhibition efficiency was the highest and the corrosion rate was the lowest when the ratio of ammonium citrate and methionine was 90:10. The adsorption isotherm revealed that the as-obtained inhibitors could tightly cover on the steel surface by physicochemical interaction.
Significant findings This work puts forward the corrosion protection mechanism of N, S-CDs on metal, which can provide technical guidance and basic theories for new corrosion inhibitors in metal corrosion protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N, S-CDs
N:S ratio
Corrosion rate
Inhibition efficiency

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structure and topography information of inhibitor (a) FTIR, (b) UV-Vis, (c) SPM-N-CDs, (d) SPM-N, S-CDs, (e) SPM-N-CDs, (f) SPM-N, S-CDs.
OCP curves after adding inhibitors (a) N-CDs, (b) N, S5%-CDs, (c) N, S10%-CDs, (d) N, S15%-CDs.
EIS results of steel in HCl solutions with various inhibitors after 1 day immersion (a)(b) N-CDs, (c)(d) N, S5%-CDs, (e)(f) N, S10%-CDs, (g)(h) N, S15%-CDs.
The equivalent circuit of corrosion process.
The variation of Rp and Cdl values as a function of immersion time (a) Rp value, (b) Cdl value.
Tafel curves after 1 day immersion under acid solution with various inhibitors (a) N-CDs, (b) N, S5%-CDs, (c) N, S10%-CDs, (d) N, S15%-CDs.

Corrosion morphology of metal surface after 24 h immersion (a) blank -12 h, (b) blank -1 day, (c) 50 mg/L-N-CDs-1 day, (d) 200 mg/L- N-CDs -1 day, (e) 50 mg/L-N, S5%-CDs-1 day, (f) 200 mg/L-N, S5%-CDs-1 day, (g) 50 mg/L-N, S10%-CDs-1 day, (h) 200 mg/L-N, S10%-CDs-1 day, (i) 50 mg/L-N, S15%-CDs-1 day, (j) 200 mg/L-N, S15%-CDs-1 day.

3D interface morphology and roughness after 24 h immersion (a) blank -12 h, (b) blank -1 day, (c) 50 mg/L-N-CDs-1 day, (d) 200 mg/L- N-CDs -1 day, (e) 50 mg/L-N, S5%-CDs-1 day, (f) 200 mg/L-N, S5%-CDs-1 day, (g) 50 mg/L-N, S10%-CDs-1 day, (h) 200 mg/L-N, S10%-CDs-1 day, (i) 50 mg/L-N, S15%-CDs-1 day, (j) 200 mg/L-N, S15%-CDs-1 day.
Corrosion rate of specimen after immersion under different (a) concentrations and (b) soaking time.
Adsorption isotherm of specimen during corrosion process (a) N-CDs, (b) N, S5%-CDs, (c) N, S10%-CDs, (d) N, S15%-CDs.

head.ce:title: The effect of N and S ratios in N, S co-doped carbon dot inhibitor on metal protection in 1 M HCl solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, Jiangxi 341000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Metallurgy and Chemistry
Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 400045, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCollege of Materials Science and Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 400045, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, Jiangxi 341000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cJiangxi University of Science and Technology, Ganzhou, Jiangxi 341000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The paper article wants to develop a new type of corrosion inhibitor to replace the traditional corrosion inhibitor and reduce environmental pollution.

A kind of high-performance N, S co-doped carbon dot (N, S-CDs) inhibitor was synthesized by ammonium citrate and methionine with different ratios (100:0, 95:5, 90:10, 85:15). A series of detection equipment were selected to evaluate the structure and corrosion inhibition behavior of as-obtained inhibitors. The result showed that the as-obtained inhibitors could effectively enhance the impedance of steel in acid environment. With the increase of addition amount, the enhancement degree was increased. In the meantime, the icorr of steel was decreased 1∼2 orders of magnitude in acid solution after adding 200 mg/L inhibitor. Among them, the corrosion inhibition efficiency was the highest and the corrosion rate was the lowest when the ratio of ammonium citrate and methionine was 90:10. The adsorption isotherm revealed that the as-obtained inhibitors could tightly cover on the steel surface by physicochemical interaction.
This work puts forward the corrosion protection mechanism of N, S-CDs on metal, which can provide technical guidance and basic theories for new corrosion inhibitors in metal corrosion protection.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal corrosion refers to the damage, fracture, perforation or abrasion of material caused by the influence of the surrounding medium on the metal in the external environment [1]. Metal corrosion will not only affect the decline of metal mechanical functions, but also bring catastrophic accidents and cause economic losses [2–4]. The latest corrosion survey results show that the annual direct economic loss of world caused by metal corrosion is about 700–1000 billion dollars [5]. Especially in industrially developed countries, the direct economic loss caused by metal corrosion accounts for about 2% to 4% of the national economic product, and the indirect loss caused by it is almost incalculable [6]. In the meantime, metal corrosion not only limits the development and progress of science and technology, but also pollutes the environment and affects the normal rhythm of human life [7–9].

Corrosion inhibitor is a chemical substance, which can significantly reduce the corrosion rate of metal by adding appropriate concentration to the corrosive environment without changing the physical and mechanical properties of metal [10–16]. In recent years, with the enhancement of the people consciousness of environment protection and the requirements for the sustainability of social development, the development of high-efficiency, non-toxic, harmless, and easily biodegradable corrosion inhibitors has gradually become one of the current research and development directions. Among them, carbon dots have become the current research hotspot of corrosion inhibitors because of their high efficiency, long-lasting and environmental protection [17–20]. For instance, Lv et al. [21] synthesized new N-CDs based on low-cost products such urotropine, citric acid, and diethylenetriamine by microwave syntheses methodology. These substances revealed super inhibition function to steel in gas and oil industry. Liu et al. [22] introduced three new N-CDs under an HCl solution. It was found that the maximum inhibition protection degrees of N-CDs were 94.23% (150 °C), 97.43% (180 °C) and 90.32% (210 °C) at 200 mg/L, respectively. Keerthana and Ashraf [23] introduced a new N-CDs corrosion inhibitor for carbon steel in 3.5% NaCl solution, and the corrosion inhibition effect for metal was excellent. Luo et al. [24] discussed the influence of preparation parameter on the inhibition function of N-CDs, and displayed that 2 h and 180 °C were the ideal conditions.

Considering the good corrosion inhibition efficiency, eco-friendly nature and water solubility of carbon dots, the co-doping of N and S atoms were studied to enhance the inhibition behavior of CDs. Cen et al. [25] selected aminosalicylic acid and thiourea to obtain N, S-CDs, and confirmed that the N, S-CDs were excellent corrosion inhibitors for metal at low concentration in harsh environment. Chen et al. [26] obtained N, S-CDs by hydrothermal method and investigated its inhibition performance for steel metal in 0.1 M HCl solution. The nitrogen atoms, hydroxyl and amino functional groups were the main reasons for the chemisorption of inhibitor on the metal surface. Saraswat and Yadav researched [27] the inhibition efficiency of N-CDs and N, S-CDs for mild steel in hydrochloric solution and found the value of N, S-CDs (96.4%) was higher than N-CDs (90.0%) at 100 mg /L. However, the influence of N and S ratio on the protecting effect of N, S-CDs has not been reported.

To endow the green inhibitor with excellent inhibition effect, we designed a N, S co-doped CDs inhibitor with various N and S ratios, which composed by the ammonium citrate and methionine. The corrosion behavior and protective mechanism of N, S-CDs with various N and S ratios in HCl solution were clearly clarified.

In this research work, Q235 carbon steel was selected for all experiments. The selected metal samples were composited as follows: 0.19 wt.% C, 0.31 wt.% Si, 0.45 wt.% Mn, 0.03 wt.% S, 0.02 wt.% P and 99 wt.% Fe. 1 M H+ and 1 M Cl– solution with various inhibitors (0, 50, 100, 150, 200 mg/L) was chosen as the corrosive solution, which was prepared from 1 M HCl solution. Ammonium citrate and methionine were the main raw materials for the preparation of corrosion inhibitors.

N, S-CDs were obtained through hydrothermal synthesis method and the schematic diagram was shown in Fig. 1 . Ammonium citrate and methionine with different ratios (100:0, 95:5, 90:10, 85:15) were calculated and the total quality was 2 g. For convenience, four corrosion inhibitors were abbreviated as N-CDs, N, S5%-CDs, N, S10%-CDs, N, S15%-CDs. Then, it was dissolved in distilled water and the solution was transferred to the reaction still, the reaction parameters were 180 °C and 2 h. After the reaction was finish, the mixture was purified and dried under certain conditions.

FTIR and UV-Vis were chosen to illustrate the structure of as-prepared inhibitor. The morphological characteristics and dimensions of as-prepared inhibitor were clarified through SPM. The corrosion inhibition behaviors after adding various N, S-CDs inhibitors were evaluated by EIS and Tafel tests. CHI660E electrochemical workstation was employed to characterize the EIS information. A three-electrode cell assembly, which contained a reference electrode (saturated calomel), counter electrode (platinum) and working electrode (carbon steel), was used in all electrochemical experiments. The frequency of 100 kHz∼0.01 Hz and the amplitude sinusoidal voltage of 20 mV were used for EIS measurements. The polarization curves were carried out in -250 mV∼ +250 mV versus SCE and the potential sweep rate of 0.5 mV s−1. After these tests, the corrosion situation and roughness were tested through SEM and LSCM. Meanwhile, the corrosion rate (Crate) was obtained according to NACE TM0169/G31 standard [28].

Fig. 2 revealed the structure and topography information of N-CDs and N, S-CDs inhibitors. For FTIR spectrum, as seen in Fig. 2a, the stretching vibration peaks of N-CDs at 1296.1, 1436.9, 1538.1, 2836.2, 3402.3 cm−1 corresponded C-O, C-N, C=N, C-H, N-H. In the meantime, the peaks at 1647.3 as well as 1693.9 cm−1 were attributed to -CO-NH- and C=O [29]. Compared with N-CDs, the new peaks at 1383.9 and 1157 cm−1 belonging to C–S and -SO3 or C=S were detected in N, S-CDs, implying the successful doping of N and S atoms, which was well in agreement with previous study [30, 31]. In terms of UV-Vis spectrum, as seen in Fig. 2b, a absorbance peak at 280 nm was observed in as-prepared N-CDs inhibitor, which was assigned to π-π* electronic transition. However, two peaks were detected in N, S-CDs inhibitor. Expect for π-π* electronic transition, the new peak at 350 nm belonged to n-π* transition [32,33]. In the case of SPM image, the N-CDs and N, S-CDs inhibitors were granular and showed a relatively uniform distribution (Fig. 2c and d). By measurement, the average size of N-CDs inhibitor was about 2–6 nm (Fig. 2e). After the co-doping of N and S atoms, the average size of N, S-CDs inhibitor was about 4∼8 nm (Fig. 2f).

The OCP curve with and without inhibitors was revealed in Fig. 3 . Overall, as the increase of inhibitor addition amount, the stable OCP value of steel in HCl solution transferred to the positive direction, indicating the inhibition of corrosion tendency caused by as-prepared inhibitor. At the same parameter, as the increase of S addition amount, the stable OCP value of steel in HCl solution showed an uptrend, indicating that the corrosion inhibition tendency of steel was reduced after doping S atoms. However, the OCP showed an uptrend until N, S10%-CDs and then decreased as the S addition amount further increased. For example, the stable OCP value in HCl environment was about -0.541 V and then increased to -0.514 V at 200 mg/L of N-CDs. At the same concentration, the stable OCP value reached the highest value of -0.438 V at 200 mg/L of N, S10%-CDs.

Fig. 4 displayed the EIS results of steel in HCl solutions with inhibitors after 1 day immersion. As our previous study, the diameter of capacitive arc was closely related to the corrosion resistance of substance [34]. Obviously, the diameter of capacitive arc was positively correlated with the concentration of corrosion inhibitor. By contrast, the diameter of capacitive arc in inhibitor solution was larger than that in blank solution. In the meantime, the diameter of capacitive arc in N, S-CDs environment was higher than that in N-CDs environment. The low-frequency impedance at 0.01 Hz (|Z|0.01Hz) could be used for effective evaluation of film resistance. It was observed in Fig. 4b that the steel exhibited a low |Z|0.01Hz (9.3 Ω•cm2) in blank acid solution, indicating the poor resistance for corrosion ion during immersion test. In N-CDs environment, the |Z|0.01Hz increased from 130 Ω•cm2 at 50 mg/L to 1180 Ω•cm2 at 200 mg/L, showing an obvious increasing trend. After co-doping N and S atoms, the |Z|0.01Hz increased from 290 to 1300 Ω•cm2 as the concentration of N, S5%-CDs increased from 50 to 200 mg/L (Fig. 4d). At the same concentration, the |Z|0.01Hz value exhibited an uptrend until N, S10%-CDs and then reduced as the increase of S and N ratio (Fig. 4f). At 200 mg/L, the |Z|0.01Hz value reached up to 2210 Ω•cm2, which was 237.6 and 1.87 times larger than blank and N-CDs environments. These EIS results revealed that the addition of N, S-CDs into acid solution could dramatically strengthen the corrosion resistance of metal. In terms of phase angle, as seen in Fig. 4c, the largest phase angle and its range were the lowest. After adding inhibitor, the range was enlarged and revealed an uptrend with inhibitor addition amount increased (Fig. 4c, f, i, l). The phenomenon also implied the adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the surface of steel, thus giving it an effective protective ability to steel [35]. Among all corrosion inhibitors, the N, S10%-CDs inhibitor showed the best protection effect.

Based on the EIS information, Fig. 5 displayed the equivalent circuit. Among all parameters, Rs was the solution resistance, Rp (=Rf+ Rct) and Cdl represented the polarization resistance and constant phase element of double layer, respectively. By calculation, the fitting result of these parameters was listed in Table 1 . Clearly, the value of Rs was similar. Due to the Rp value was one important parameter to reflect the absorption film resistance against the corrosion ion and the resistance of electron transfer on metal surface. It was clearly seen that the Rp value showed an uptrend as the increase of inhibitor addition amount during the whole test period, verifying the improvement of anti-corrosion ability after adding inhibitor. In addition, the increase of Rp value could indirectly reflect the change of adsorption film thickness and the coverage area caused by inhibitor. By contrast, the Rp value in N, S-CDs solution was higher than that in N-CDs environment, implying the decrease of adsorption difficulty on metal surfaces. Meanwhile, the Rp value in N, S10%-CDs solution was higher than other inhibitors. However, the Cdl showed an opposite change trend to Rp as S and N ratio changed. For deviation index (n), a lower value implied a rougher interface. By comparison, the value was the lowest in blank solution and was the highest in N, S10%-CDs solution.

The change of Rp and Cdl values under different immersion time could be used to evaluate the high efficiency of inhibitor. Fig. 6 revealed the variation of Rp and Cdl values as a function of immersion time. With the immersion time increased from 2 to 1 day, the Rp value in HCl environment was the lowest and revealed a stable trend (Fig. 6a). But the value in inhibitor solution showed a rising trend, indicating the increase of adsorption film thickness and coverage area with time. For instance, the Rp under 200 mg/L N-CDs environment after 1 day immersion was about 1205 Ω•cm2, which was 2.8, 1.9 and 1.4 times higher than 2, 6 and 12 h immersion. After the co-doping of N and S atoms, the Rp value in all immersion time reached the highest value at N, S10%-CDs. In this case, the Rp value was about 736, 1078, 1863 and 2400 Ω•cm2 at 2, 6, 12 h and 1 day immersion, which was 71.1%, 73.9%, 116.6% and 99.2% higher than that in N-CDs solution. For Cdl value, the value was the highest in the absence of inhibitors and revealed a downtrend as the immersion time increased (Fig. 6b). After adding inhibitor, the Cdl value was greatly reduced, indicating that the electron transfer path on metal surface was inhibited. As S and N ratio enlarged, the Cdl exhibited an uptrend and then slightly reduced, which revealed the minimum at 10:90, implying the strongest inhibition ability for steel in corrosive medium.

The polarization test was utilized to evaluate the corrosion rate of steels and the inhibition mechanism of inhibitor. Fig. 7 illustrated the result of tafel curve. Through calculation, the related parameters from Tafel curve were listed Table 2 . These parameters from left to right were corrosion potential, corrosion current density, polarization resistance, anodic Tafel slope (βa), cathodic Tafel slope (βc), surface coverage (θ) as well as inhibitive efficiency (IE). From Eqs. (1) and (2), the θ as well as IE were calculated [36,37]:
(1)
θ =
i
c o r r
0
−
i
c o r r
i
c o r r
0
(2)
I E =
i
c o r r
0
−
i
c o r r
i
c o r r
0
× 100 %
where the corrosion current density in blank solution with and without N, S-CDs was expressed as i°corr and icorr. As a whole, the corrosion potential was reduced in HCl solution after adding inhibitors. However, the current density of steel showed a downtrend as the increase of inhibitor addition amount, which was attributed to the inhibitor adsorption on the interface between steel and medium. In the meantime, the steel showed the minimum icorr value at methionine and ammonium citrate of 10:90. For Rp value, the change trend was opposite to the icorr value. At 200 mg/L, the Rp value was about 1214.9, 1588.3, 2411.4, and 2107.3 Ω•cm2 in N-CDs, N, S5%-CDs, N, S10%-CDs and N, S15%-CDs solution. For Tafel slope, both βa and βc values revealed an obvious changes after adding and increasing the inhibitor, indicating that the as-prepared carbon dots were mixed corrosion inhibitors [35]. For θ and IE, the variation was similar with Rp value. For instance, at the methionine and ammonium citrate ratio of 0:100, the IE ranged from 75% at 50 mg/L to 91% at 200 mg/L. As the methionine and ammonium citrate ratio increased from 0:100 to 15:75, the IE increased from 91% to 98% and then decreased to 96% at 200 mg/L. By comparison, the IE reached the highest at N, S10%-CDs. Therefore, the methionine and ammonium citrate ratio of 10:90 was the best parameter, thereby making a shield against the attack of corrosion ion to give inhibition function for corrosion under aggressive solution. Furthermore, through the analysis in Table S1, the N, S10%-CDs revealed a better corrosion protection performance to metal than other carbon dots-based inhibitors in corrosion medium [22,24,27,38–45].

After immersion test, Fig. 8 showed the corrosion morphology of interface. In blank solution, some corrosion pores and furrows were observed on the steel after immersion 12 h (Fig. 8a). With the prolonging of immersion time (1 day), the furrows and cracks became more obvious, indicating the serious corrosion (Fig. 8b). When the N-CDs addition amount was 50 mg/L, the corrosion sign was slightly improved after 1 day immersion (Fig. 8c). With the increase of N-CDs addition amount, the signs of corrosion were alleviated. (Fig. 8d). As the methionine and ammonium citrate ratio increased from 0:100 to 10:90, a smooth interface was observed, hinting the good inhibitive effect (Fig. 8c–h). Nevertheless, the signs of corrosion on the metal surface began to aggravate as the methionine and ammonium citrate ratio continued to rise (Fig. 8i and j), showing that the change of methionine and ammonium citrate ratio was more sensitive to the inhibition effect.

Fig. 9 was the 3D surface morphology as well as surface toughness of interface after immersion. In blank environment, the steel surface was coarse because of the attack of acid and the surface roughness was about 2.39 μm after 12 h immersion (Fig. 9a). After 1 day immersion, many bumps were observed on the steel surface and the surface roughness increased to 2.67 μm (Fig. 9b). When the N-CDs addition amount was 50 mg/L, the bump region was slightly suppressed and the surface roughness decreased to 1.18 μm, corresponding to the decline of corrosion signs (Fig. 9c). When the addition amount was 200 mg/L, the bump region was further shrunk and the surface roughness decreased to 0.73 μm, explaining the formation of N-CDs protective film (Fig. 9d). After adding 50 and 200 mg/L of N, S5%-CDs, the surface roughness was 0.89 and 0.62 μm, which was further reduced in comparison to N-CDs, implying the in-depth inhibition of corrosion on the basis of N doping (Fig. 9e and f). As the methionine and ammonium citrate ratio increased to 10:90, the steel surface became smoother and the surface roughness reduced to 0.48 μm at 200 mg/L, which was 82% lower than that in blank solution (Fig. 9h). However, a slight increase of surface roughness was observed as the methionine and ammonium citrate ratio continued to rise (Fig. 9i and j), suggesting that the S and N ratio was a key factor in determining the corrosion interface roughness.

Fig. 10 displayed the Crate of bare steel after immersion in various conditions. In blank environment, the Crate was 23.2 mm/year after immersion 1 day. When the N-CDs, N, S5%-CDs, N, S10%-CDs and N, S15%-CDs addition amount was 50 mg/L, the Crate reduced to 13.1, 10.6, 9.4, and 9.9 mm/year, which was 43%, 56%, 60% and 67% lower than that under HCl environment, also confirming the corrosion inhibitive function of inhibitor. Along with inhibitor addition amount rose, the Crate value exhibited a decreasing trend until 200 mg/L. Under different methionine and ammonium citrate ratios, the S and N ratio greatly affected the Crate value. Through calculation, the Crate reduced first and then increased along with the methionine and ammonium citrate ratio decreased. And the Crate was 7.8, 7.3, 4.7 and 5.9 mm/year at 200 mg/L of N-CDs, N, S5%-CDs, N, S10%-CDs and N, S15%-CDs. Thus, the lowest Crate was obtained at methionine and ammonium citrate ratio of 10:90. In addition, as the immersion time prolonged, the Crate value was obviously increased in blank solution. Nevertheless, the Crate was slightly decreased after introducing N-CDs and greatly decreased after adding N, S10%-CDs.

Fig. 11 displayed the adsorption isotherm during corrosion process. From the result, the fitted degree was about 0.998, 1, 0.999 and 0.999, implying an ideal linear relation between C/θ and C. Moreover, from Eqs. (3) and (4), the Kads and ΔG0 ads were calculated [46,47]:
(3)
θ
1 − θ
=
K
a d s
C
(4)
Δ
G
a d s
0
= − R T I n
(
1000
K
a d s
)
where θ denoted surface coverage, C represented inhibitor concentration, and Kads expressed as adsorption equilibrium constant, R was 8.314 J/mol−1K−1 and T was 298 K. From previous study, the high Kads hinted strong adsorption strength [48]. Obviously, the K values at ammonium citrate and methionine ratio of 100:0, 95:5, 90:10, 85:15 were about 58.75, 152.67, 164.20 and 161.29 L/g, suggesting the adsorption strength was the strongest at 90:10. For ΔG0 ads value, when ΔG0 ads ≥ −20 kJ mol−1, it was physical adsorption. When ΔG0 ads ≤ -40 kJ mol−1, it was chemical adsorption [49]. Through calculation, the ΔG0 ads value of inhibitor prepared at ammonium citrate and methionine ratio of 100:0, 95:5, 90:10, 85:15 was about -27.21, -29.57, -29.75, and -29.71 kJ mol−1, respectively. Therefore, the adsorption nature of as-prepared inhibitor in HCl solution was physical and chemical co-adsorption.

The protection mechanism schematic was shown in Fig. 12 . Combined with previous study, the protective of N, S-CDs inhibitor mainly contained two parts of physical adsorption and chemical adsorption. Among them, the physical adsorption was from the functional groups (such as hydroxyl and carbonyl groups) on N, S-CDs. These behaviors presented a great influence on the adsorption of electrostatic attraction, which resulted in the adsorption of N, S-CDs on steel surface and then prevented the electrochemical reaction. Moreover, some functional groups of N, S-CDs could promote their own agglomeration, thereby isolating aggressive medium. The chemical adsorption of N, S-CDs inhibitor was due to the reaction between heteroatoms (such as N and S) and steel. The bonding strength of chemical bonds was significantly higher than that of physical bonding, which was conducive to reduce the number of active sites. Moreover, the N, S-CDs could enhance the hydrophobic ability of interface, which could effectively avoid the contact of corrosion medium [25]. Thus, the protective film could suppress the attack of H+ and Cl−.

Through the reaction between methionine and ammonium citrate, a green and excellent inhibitor was obtained. The corrosion and weight loss behaviors of metal under 1 M H+ and 1 M Cl– environment after introducing N-CDs and N, S-CDs were studied. As inhibitor addition amount increased, the impedance modulus and inhibition efficiency showed an uptrend, the current density and corrosion rate revealed a downtrend. Meanwhile, the N, S-CDs displayed a stronger protection for steel against corrosion in comparison to N-CDs. By analysis, the methionine and ammonium citrate ratio played a strong impact on the protective behavior of N, S-CDs. The resistance of the metal to corrosion was the largest at methionine and ammonium citrate radio of 10:90. Meanwhile, the IE of N, S10%-CDs reached up to 98% at 200 mg/L, exhibiting a good protective effect, which was due to the physical and chemical co-adsorption of inhibitor.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Scientific Research Foundation of Jiangxi University of Science and Technology (No.205200100421); Jiangxi Provincial Department of Education (No. GJJ190469); Program of Qingjiang Excellent Young Talents, Jiangxi University of Science and Technology (JXUSTQJYX2020010); Jiangxi Province ‘‘Double Thousand Plan” Innovation Leading Talent Short-term Project (jxsq2020007); Bureau of Science and Technology of Ganzhou Municipality; The Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program for College Students in Jiangxi Province; Distinguished Professor Program of Jinggang Scholars in institutions of higher learning, Jiangxi Province; Jiangxi Province ‘‘Double Thousand Plan” Science and Technology Innovation high-end Talent Project (jxsq2019201039).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2021.08.023.
